risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Participants were randomized to either the hydro-cortisone (n = 31: 21 males, 10 females) or placebo (n = 33: 21 males, 12 females) groups." Comment: The paper states that participants were randomized, but it does not describe the method used to generate the allocation sequence (e.g., computer-generated numbers, random number table, coin toss). Under Cochrane criteria, "randomized" alone is insufficient to judge low risk.

Summary: The paper states that participants were "randomly assigned, in double-blind fashion" to hydrocortisone or placebo, but it gives no explicit description of how the allocation sequence was concealed from the enrolling nurse/recruiter (e.g., central allocation, SNOSE, identical containers). Comment: Randomization and blinding are reported, but these do not establish concealment of upcoming assignments before enrollment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "participants were consented in-hospital and randomly assigned, in double-blind fashion, to either a 10-day course ... of hydrocortisone or placebo" and "Following consent, the nurse administered the first oral dose [20 mg hydrocortisone ... or placebo capsules]". Comment: The study explicitly states it was double-blind and used placebo capsules, indicating participants and personnel delivering the medication were intended to be blinded. The main outcomes (PTSD symptoms, depression, and quality of life) could in principle be influenced by knowledge of allocation, but the reported blinding makes performance bias unlikely.

Quote: "Participants were administered the Clinician-Administered PTSD Scale (CAPS)51 to assess incidence of PTSD and PTSD symptoms..." and "randomly assigned, in double-blind fashion, to either a 10-day course... of hydrocortisone or placebo." Comment: The key outcome assessor for PTSD was the clinician administering the CAPS, which is an independent clinician-rated interview. The paper explicitly states the trial was double-blind, and there is no indication that the clinician outcome assessor knew allocation. The other outcomes (CES-D and SF-36) were self-report, but the main assessed outcome here is clinician-administered PTSD assessment; with double-blind allocation and a clinician interview outcome, detection bias is unlikely.

Quote: "Fifty-one participants (78%) were retained at the 1-month follow-up, and 42 (65%) were retained at the 3-month follow-up. There was no differential drop out between the hydrocortisone and placebo groups. At 1 month posttrauma, 6 participants had dropped from the placebo group and 7 had dropped from the hydrocortisone group, while at 3 months a total of 9 had dropped from the placebo group and 12 had dropped from the hydrocortisone group... Drop-outs between the 1- and 3- month time points were all due to inability to reach participants after repeated attempts. There were no differences between drop-outs and participants who were retained through the protocol on any study variable." Comment: Attrition was moderate but balanced across groups, reasons were not outcome-related, and the authors report no baseline differences between dropouts and completers; this makes attrition bias unlikely despite the completer analyses and lack of formal imputation.
